title: How do you change a company's direction with one prototype?
subtitle: Using user research to overturn executive assumptions at a print-on-demand platform serving hundreds of thousands
team: 3 → growing to larger implementation teams, Leipzig

hero_image.alt: How do you change a company's direction with one prototype? — abstract resolution-field cover

sections.headline: How do you change a company's direction with one prototype?
sections.subtext: The management vision was a 60-second automated journey. User research said something completely different. Here's how data won.

images.alt: Spreadshirt platform — design your own custom t-shirt
images.caption: Spreadshirt — where hundreds of thousands design custom products

sections.headline: The Starting Point
sections.content:
Spreadshirt was running on a product assumption that felt right at the top: shop owners wanted speed. The leadership vision was a 60-second fully automated journey — pick a template, customize automatically, ship it. Efficient. Scalable. Clean.

The company had tried to redesign the seller experience twice before and failed both times. I was brought in to lead the third attempt.

Initial question: what if the assumption was wrong?

sections.quote: The t-shirt designer is the most uplifting experience I have all day.
sections.context: This single sentence changed the product strategy

sections.headline: The Discovery
sections.content:
I went out and talked to actual shop owners. Not surveys, not analytics — conversations.

What I found completely contradicted the leadership vision. Shop owners didn't want the design process automated away. For them, the t-shirt designer wasn't a friction point — it was the most joyful part of their day. They valued design control over speed. The creative process was the product.

This wasn't a small insight. It meant the entire 60-second automation strategy was headed in the wrong direction.

sections.headline: The Hard Part
sections.content:
The data said one thing. The executive team's intuition said another. And this wasn't the first time the company had tried this redesign — two previous attempts had already failed.

I needed to make the research undeniable. A presentation wouldn't work. I needed to show, not tell.

So I built a prototype — a mobile version of the t-shirt designer — and put it directly in users' hands. The goal wasn't to prove mobile was possible. It was to demonstrate that the design experience itself was worth preserving, even on a small screen.

steps.label: User Interviews
steps.description: Conducted in-depth conversations with shop owners, revealing that the design process was the emotional core of their experience — not friction to be removed.

steps.label: Research Prototype
steps.description: Built a mobile t-shirt designer prototype in Axure RP, developed in collaboration with UI designers over one intensive week. Designed specifically to elicit user feedback, not for shipping.

image.caption: One-week mobile prototype — proving desktop functionality could work on a small screen

steps.label: Executive Workshops
steps.description: Facilitated workshops with the executive team and business unit leadership, presenting research findings that contradicted leadership assumptions about automation.

steps.label: User Testing
steps.description: Tested the mobile prototype with real users. Feedback was overwhelmingly positive — demonstrating that the full desktop experience could be adapted for mobile.

sections.headline: Results

metrics.label: Product Strategy
metrics.before: 60-second fully automated journey — eliminate design friction
metrics.after: Controlled automation — preserve user agency and creative control

metrics.label: Mobile Prototype
metrics.value: "Resoundingly positive" user feedback → moved to production implementation

metrics.label: Redesign Project
metrics.after: 9-month project completed successfully, team grew from 3 to full implementation

metrics.label: Executive Buy-in
metrics.value: Secured leadership support for direction contradicting their original intuition

sections.narrative: The mobile prototype succeeded — it showed that the design experience could work beautifully on mobile. User testing feedback was "resoundingly positive." But the bigger impact was strategic: leadership changed the fundamental product approach from full automation to controlled automation. The project that had failed twice before completed successfully over 9 months, eventually scaling from a 3-person team to larger implementation teams.

sections.headline: What I Learned
sections.content:
This experience taught me that data is not enough — you need to make it undeniable. When research contradicts leadership intuition, a presentation won't change minds. A prototype that puts users in control will.

It also reinforced that the best product strategy doesn't start with efficiency metrics — it starts with understanding what users actually value. Sometimes the thing you're trying to optimize away is the thing they love most.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If your team is headed in a direction that feels right but might be wrong, someone needs to talk to the users before more resources are committed. I can lead that conversation — and I can make the findings stick through research that speaks louder than any slide deck.

executive.summary: Changed a company's product strategy from full automation to controlled automation by conducting user research that revealed shop owners valued creative control above all else. Prototype succeeded, project completed after 2 prior failures.
executive.angle: How user research overturned executive intuition

narrative.summary: What happens when user research contradicts leadership intuition? This is the story of how one shop owner's quote changed an entire company's product direction.
